name: The Timing Karee Wrote Down
description: Karee has been recording the times each night when the trench crystal warms. Eight months of data. Serel won't hear it. She wants a cross-faction carrier to take it to Pilot Kira.
accept_text: She pulls a folded paper from her jacket — not the official notebook, a separate document she's been keeping on her own. 'Eight months,' she says. 'Starting the night the warmings began. I've been recording the time every night — when the trench crystal changes, when it brightens, how long the warming lasts.' She holds out the paper. 'The intervals are consistent. There's drift, but the pattern fits on a curve. A regular one.' She checks the sergeant's position — the automatic check, three hundred meters south, not there. 'Serel says it's a known phenomenon. He won't say what phenomenon.' She looks at the crystal stake. 'Kira is Skyborn Fleet. She's been mapping the lattice pattern below the Wastes from altitude. I know this because I read Serel's informal communications when he's not watching them, which I'm not supposed to do.' She doesn't apologize. 'If the ground-level timing matches the lattice rhythm she's mapping from above — if what I've been recording here corresponds to what she can see from eight thousand meters — that's not two observations. That's one thing, viewed from two angles.' She meets your eyes. 'Serel won't send it up the chain. He doesn't want either government asking questions about the Grid until he understands more.' A pause. 'Will you take it to Kira? She'll know what it means faster than either of us.'
complete_text: Kira unfolds Karee's paper and reads the timing data the way she reads navigational instruments — as information that tells you where you are relative to where you're going. She is quiet. Then she pulls her own instrument log and places it beside the paper. 'The intervals match,' she says. Not surprised — the tone of a hypothesis that has been confirmed and that the confirming person is not entirely certain they wanted confirmed this specifically. 'Every warming Karee recorded corresponds to a lattice coherence peak in my altitude readings. To the hour.' She looks at both documents. 'The trench crystal and the lattice are on the same rhythm.' She marks a position on her chart with one precise mark. 'I've been trying to locate the node boundaries from altitude for eight months and couldn't get fine enough resolution. Karee's data gives me the position.' She looks up. 'She's been sitting on a Grid node for eight months without knowing it. The pulse she's been recording is the construction wave cycling through active nodes as they come online. When the wave reaches her node, the trench crystal warms.' She folds the paper carefully and puts it in her log. 'I'm not giving this back. Ask her if she'll keep recording. Tell her: the sergeant's official position is that it's a known phenomenon. Tell her the unofficial position is that she's the only ground-level observer tracking the Grid's heartbeat with this consistency, and it matters.'
